cards.content:
**Core concept:** CPR generates forward flow via intrathoracic pressure rise during compression and venous return via negative pressure on recoil; survival peaks at optimal depth/rate.

**Key parameters:** Depth 40-50 mm (5 cm), rate 100-120/min; <5 mm or rate <100/>120 worsens outcome. Minimize pauses; decompress fully.

**Clinical application:** Guide real-time compressor feedback and rotation every 2 min to maintain perfusion.

**Interventions:** 30:2 if no advanced airway; once secured, 10 breaths/min. Avoid excessive ventilation (↑ RV afterload, ↓ venous return).

**Pitfalls:** Inadequate depth, excessive rate (>120) limiting filling, and hyperventilation causing cerebral vasoconstriction are common errors.

**Core concept:** Shockable (VF/pVT) vs non-shockable (PEA/asystole) pathways dictate defibrillation and epinephrine timing.

**Key parameters:** Biphasic 200J, monophasic 360J. Epinephrine IV/IO every 3-5 min (shockable) or ASAP (non-shockable). ROSC: PETCO2 ≥40 mmHg.

**Clinical application:** Direct rhythm-specific actions; reversible causes (H&T) addressed concurrently.

**Interventions:** Amiodarone 300 mg then 150 mg; lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg then 0.5-0.75 mg/kg. Quantitative capnography to assess CPR quality.

**Pitfalls:** Delayed epinephrine in PEA, failing to treat H&T, and misinterpretation of low PETCO2 as ROSC rather than poor compressions.

**Core concept:** Post-resuscitation care optimizes organ perfusion and limits secondary injury after ROSC.

**Key parameters:** SBP ≥90 mmHg, MAP >65 mmHg; SpO2 92-98%, PaCO2 35-45 mmHg. Obtain 12-lead ECG and labs immediately.

**Clinical application:** Targets guide fluid and vasopressor use and identify ACS requiring emergent intervention.

**Interventions:** Crystalloids + vasopressors; early intubation with waveform capnography; emergent PCI if STEMI or cardiogenic shock.

**Pitfalls:** Hyperoxia/hypocapnia worsen outcomes; neglecting coronary etiology; hypotension untreated leading to recurrent arrest.

**Core concept:** In comatose post-arrest patients, temperature control mitigates brain injury; hypothermia vs normothermia equivalence shown in shockable rhythms.

**Key parameters:** Initiate <36°C maintenance 24h; rewarm 0.25-0.5°C/h. TTM2: normothermia <37.5°C non-inferior to 33°C.

**Clinical application:** Comatose patients get TTM; non-shockable may benefit from 33°C (HYPERION).

**Interventions:** Surface/intravascular cooling, cold saline; sedation minimal; monitor coagulopathy, arrhythmias. Avoid propofol/fentanyl interfering neuro exam.

**Pitfalls:** Overcooling complications; delayed rewarming; using hypothermia in awake patients; neuroprognostication before 72h post-rewarm.

**Core concept:** Pre-arrest factors and rhythm predict survival; termination criteria avoid futile transport.

**Key parameters:** CREST (0-5): CAD, non-shockable, EF<30%, shock, time>25min; score 5 →50% survival. Termination if unwitnessed, no CPR, no ROSC, no shock.

**Clinical application:** Risk-stratify and counsel; shockable rhythm better than asystole/PEA.

**Interventions:** None specific; guide ECMO consideration if refractory.

**Pitfalls:** Premature neuroprognostication <72h; applying termination rules in witnessed hospital arrests; ignoring reversible causes.

**Core concept:** RCTs define TTM equivalence and ECMO/adrenaline roles.

**Key parameters:** TTM/ TTM2: 33 vs 36/37.5°C no mortality diff. HYPERION: 33°C benefits non-shockable. ARREST: ECMO for refractory VF improves discharge. PARAMEDIC-2: adrenaline ↑ survival but neuro cost.

**Clinical application:** Use normothermia widely; consider ECMO in refractory VF; adrenaline per algorithm.

**Interventions:** Early ECMO-facilitated resuscitation in OHCA refractory VF.

**Pitfalls:** Overgeneralizing HYPERION to shockable; denying adrenaline based on neuro outcome; delaying ECMO referral.

**Core concept:** Refractory arrest or post-ROSC shock may require mechanical support.

**Key parameters:** ECMO if refractory VF (ARREST) or persistent cardiogenic shock post-ROSC. H&T causes: tamponade, tension PTX, thrombosis.

**Clinical application:** Escalate to VA-ECMO when drugs/MCS insufficient; treat reversible surgically.

**Interventions:** Emergent pericardiocentesis, chest tube, PCI; ECMO cannulation. Gasping = favorable, supports venous return.

**Pitfalls:** Missing tension PTX/tamponade as reversible; ECMO delay; excessive PPV in special causes worsening hemodynamics.
